metadata.title: Why a Particle Bounces Off a Cliff It Could Easily Clear
metadata.color_semantics: claude: INK #3D3929 all text; SPARK #D97757 the one accent. Content: TEAL = transmitted wave / cleared step; CRIMSON = reflected wave / partial bounce
metadata.greeting_note: hello lexicon: Hindi. Medhavy 1-word budget. Wagwan is Bear-only.

beats.narration_text: Roll a ball off a downhill step — it keeps going, every time. Send a quantum particle at the same step with more than enough energy — part of it bounces back. Energy is not the issue. The change in wavelength at the boundary is.

props.segment: Why a Particle Bounces Off a Cliff It Could Easily Clear
props.command: why does a quantum particle reflect off a step it could clear?

props.segment: Why a Particle Bounces Off a Cliff It Could Easily Clear
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: classical ball rolling off a downhill step — speeds up on the lower side, no reflection, energy conserved"

beats.narration_text: Classical ball, downhill step: the ball speeds up on the lower side. Energy is conserved, the ball keeps rolling. Reflection is zero — there is nothing to push it back.
A quantum particle with twice the energy it needs to clear the step still reflects — not always, but sometimes. How can a wave reflect off a step it has plenty of energy to pass?

card.copy: Enough energy to clear the step. Classical: always passes. Quantum: partly bounces. What is doing the reflecting?

props.segment: Why a Particle Bounces Off a Cliff It Could Easily Clear
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: quantum wavefunction at a potential step — left side wavenumber k1, right side larger k2 after step down, show the wavelength change at the boundary"

beats.narration_text: On the left side the particle has one kinetic energy — one wavelength. On the right side, after the step, it has more kinetic energy — a shorter, faster wavelength.

props.segment: Why a Particle Bounces Off a Cliff It Could Easily Clear
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: wavefunction boundary matching — wave must be continuous and smooth at the step, this forces a split into reflected and transmitted parts"

beats.narration_text: The wave function must match at the boundary: same value and same slope on both sides. The only way to satisfy both conditions with two different wavelengths is to split — some wave reflects and some transmits.

props.segment: Why a Particle Bounces Off a Cliff It Could Easily Clear
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: impedance mismatch analogy — wave crossing from slower to faster medium, partial reflection at the interface, same mechanism as quantum step reflection"

beats.narration_text: Think of it as an impedance mismatch — the same mechanism that reflects sound at a wall or light at a glass surface. When a wave crosses into a medium with a different wave speed, some of it bounces at the interface.

props.segment: Why a Particle Bounces Off a Cliff It Could Easily Clear
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: reflection coefficient vs step abruptness — sharp step reflects more, gradual ramp reflects less, plot R vs ramp length"

beats.narration_text: The reflection coefficient depends on how abruptly the wavelength changes. A gradual slope reflects almost nothing; a sharp step reflects the most. And remarkably, a step down reflects just as well as a step up.

props.segment: Why a Particle Bounces Off a Cliff It Could Easily Clear
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: reflection formula R = ((k1-k2)/(k1+k2))^2 — plot R vs k2/k1 ratio, R=0 when wavenumbers match, R near 1 when they differ greatly"

beats.narration_text: If the step height is V-zero and the particle has energy E, the reflection goes as the ratio of the wave numbers: R equals k-one minus k-two, all squared, divided by k-one plus k-two, all squared.

props.segment: Why a Particle Bounces Off a Cliff It Could Easily Clear
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: worked example — electron 4 eV hitting step down 3 eV, k1 and k2 differ by 30%, R approximately 0.02, annotate numbers on the wavefunction plot"

beats.narration_text: Illustrative example: an electron with energy 4 eV hits a step down of 3 eV, so the far side has 7 eV of kinetic energy. The wave numbers differ by about 30 percent and roughly 2 percent of the wave reflects — completely classically forbidden.
It is not the energy that causes reflection. It is the mismatch in wavelength at the boundary. Wherever the wavelength changes suddenly, a quantum wave splits — part passes, part bounces back.

card.copy: Not the energy — the mismatch. Any sudden wavelength change splits the wave. Part passes. Part bounces.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. One electron, one step. Ask Claude to calculate how much reflects and plot R versus step height across a range of energies. Watch R and T add to one.

props.segment: Why a Particle Bounces Off a Cliff It Could Easily Clear
props.command: for a quantum particle with energy 4 eV hitting a potential step down of 3 eV, calculate R and T, then plot the reflection coefficient R vs step height for particle energies from 1 to 8 eV

beats.narration_text: If this made the mismatch click, subscribe. Medhavy.

props.title: Why a Particle Bounces Off a Cliff It Could Easily Clear.
props.subline: subscribe · the mismatch is the mirror
